title: practical english usage - Part 99
content:

others can only be used with uncountables (e.g. much,  little). Compare: Some determiners can only be used with countable nouns (e.g. many, few); not had weather (    a bad weather,three bod weathers) pure wool (xor a fnne uwdrluw pur^ wools) not clear water (     a-clear -water, two clear-waters) some exceptions (►   120.4).

plurals. We   do not normally use a/an with uncountable nouns, though there are We   cannot use numbers with uncountable nouns, and most are singular with no underwear  weather jewellery   luggage     machinery crockery   equipment   furniture baggage    clothing    cutlery oxygen     plastic    granite oil       water     steel boundaries, and not as separate objects. Some examples: qualities, collections and other things which  we  see as masses without clear Uncountable (or mass) nouns are the names of materials, liquids, abstract a cat a newspaper    three cats two newspapers nouns; they have plurals.

can be counted. We   can use numbers and the article a/an with countable Countable nouns are the names of separate objects, people, ideas, etc, which 1  the   difference basic    information 119  countable       and   uncountable         nouns: George's, Mark's, Joe's) follow the same pronunciation rules as regular plurals.

youth /ju:θ/                         youths /ju:θs/     /ju:ðz/ or wreath /ri:θ/                        wreaths /ri:θs/     /ri:ðz/ or truth /tru:θ/                      truths /tru:θs/     /tru:dz/ or roof /ru:f/                        roofs /ru:fs/      /ru:vz/ or path /pɑ:θ/                             paths /pɑ:θs/       /pa:dz/ or mouth  /maoθ/                        mouths /maoθs/      /maoðz/ or bath /bɑ:θ/                              baths /ba:θs/     /ba:ðz/ house /haos/                       houses /'haoziz/ Singular                        Plural 4 plurals     with    irregular       pronunciation Section11 Nouns     and   Noun    Phrases;     Agreement grammar «119      countable and uncountable nouns: basic information work         a piece of work; a job vocabulary    a word; an expression traffic       cars, etc thunder       a clap of thunder slang        a slang word/expression rubbish       a piece of rubbish research      a piece of research; a study; an experiment publicity      an advertisement progress      a step forward; an advance poetry       a poem news         a piece of news money        a note; a coin; a sum  of money luggage        a piece/item of luggage; a case/trunk/bag luck          a piece/bit/stroke of luck lightning       a flash of lightning knowledge     a fact information    a piece of information furniture      a piece/article of furniture; a table, chair, etc equipment      a piece of equipment; a tool, etc not chewing gum      a piece of chewing gum  (     Orchewing-gHm) chess         a game of chess bread       a piece/slice/loaf of bread; a loaf; a roll baggage      a piece/item of baggage; a case/trunk/bag not accommodation  a place to live (     an accommodation) Uncountable         Countable languages.) words that are uncountable in English have countable equivalents in other Here are some other examples of general-particular pairs.
